cards.title: Stop AI Agents From Hallucinating: The Untapped Market for Real-Time Knowledge Feeds
cards.thesis: Builders are hitting a wall with AI agents (like those in Cursor or Replit) because they're expensive and often 'hallucinate' (make up wrong information) or waste 'tokens' (the units of text AI processes). The real problem is that these agents struggle to efficiently find and use up-to-date, relevant information, leading to high costs and unreliable outputs. The next wave of valuable AI tools won't be about new models, but about making existing agents smarter and cheaper to run by feeding them better data.

evidence.text: A new tool, Semble, tackles a common issue when using AI for coding: agents often fall back to slow, token-heavy searches like 'grep' on large codebases, missing relevant code and costing a lot. Semble reduces token usage by 98%.

Booking.com is using 'vector search' (a way to find similar items quickly in huge datasets) for its agentic AI and RAG (retrieval augmented generation — where AI looks up facts before answering). They handle 250,000 partner-to-guest messages daily, showing the challenge of scaling agent knowledge.
The problem of 'AI slop' is so widespread that people are getting banned for submitting AI-generated science papers, and even Microsoft found AI degrades document quality.

One founder building a SaaS without AI features noted, 'The main issue was that the product’s “aha moment” depended too much on the AI being good. As a small founder, that is a strange position to be in.'
There's a concern that AI agents impersonate human operators by default, creating an 'illusion of human in the loop' without clear disclosure or control.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's complaining that AI coding assistants like Cursor or Replit often 'hallucinate' or use outdated information, especially about rapidly changing APIs or libraries. But nobody's made a simple plug-in or service that just auto-updates the specific documentation or knowledge bases these agents read. The first person to ship a tool that continuously scrapes and feeds fresh, verified documentation to common AI coding environments will own the frustrated-developer market, and you could build it in a weekend with a basic scraper and a vector database (a tool for efficient information retrieval).

cards.title: AI Agents Are Too Expensive & Too Spooky: Cash In On The 'AI Black Box' Panic
cards.thesis: Companies are getting hit with 'astronomical' AI bills, forcing them to scale back their spending on powerful tools like Claude. At the same time, builders are worried about what their AI agents are actually doing and what data they're sending to the cloud (meaning, off their local computer and onto a server somewhere) — leading to a massive trust and transparency gap.

evidence.text: One company is letting AI agents run radio stations and all the business operations, showing huge interest in autonomous agents, but also the inherent complexity and potential for things to go wrong.
A company is rapidly cutting AI tooling spend, with their monthly Claude bill becoming 'astronomical' — nearly 3x their cloud spend — forcing them to remove access for many employees.

People are asking, 'Do you know what data your AI coding agent sends to the cloud?' expressing concern that they have 'no idea exactly what ends up in the cloud' and if they should just 'trust the tool'.

There's a concern about whether current free/low-cost large language models (LLMs) will continue, or if high costs will push us towards slower, local models or only enterprise use, suggesting cost is a major factor for the future of AI tools.
Engineers are asking how to enforce understanding of the code they are shipping when 'everyone is using AI for everything now' and AI is writing code, creating RFCs, and even planning.

cards.opportunity: Companies are scaling back AI tool spend because bills are 'astronomical' and they're worried about what data their 'AI coding agent sends to the cloud.' What if you built a lightweight 'agent co-pilot' that doesn't just run code, but *explains* its actions, flags potential data leaks, and gives a real-time cost breakdown for every task? Ship a simple browser extension or a local proxy (a piece of software that sits between your computer and the internet, monitoring traffic) that sits between the agent and the internet, logging and estimating costs on the fly. This gives builders and companies the control and transparency they desperately need, right when they're cutting costs but still want to use AI.

cards.title: Lost Legends, New Opportunities: The Untapped Goldmine in Tech History
cards.thesis: The recent passing of computing pioneers like Peter Salus (who chronicled Unix history) and Peter Neumann (a giant in computer security) highlights a critical, often overlooked gap. The foundational stories and context behind today's tech are increasingly fading with their original creators, but understanding this history offers a powerful competitive edge for anyone building new products.

evidence.text: The Hacker News community noted the passing of Peter Salus (154 engagement), recognizing his pivotal role as a historian of Unix and the early internet, whose work captured the origin stories of much of our modern tech.

Similarly, the death of Peter Neumann (148 engagement) was marked, acknowledging his significant contributions to computer security and risk analysis, underscoring the loss of direct knowledge from early system design.

Many comments on these threads expressed a profound appreciation for the foundational work of these pioneers and lamented the loss of their unique perspectives and direct historical accounts that shaped the computing world.

cards.opportunity: The passing of computing pioneers like Peter Salus and Peter Neumann is a stark reminder that the foundational stories of how our digital world was built are often held by a shrinking group of individuals. For builders today, understanding the 'why' behind modern tech offers a massive competitive edge, but this knowledge is scattered and hard to access. Instead of just letting these stories fade, create a platform that uses AI to distill decades of historical technical papers, forum discussions, and interviews into engaging, short-form content – think interactive timelines or 'origin story' explainers for key tech concepts. First person to make computing history genuinely accessible and exciting for a new generation of builders owns the 'context' market, and you could start by picking one foundational concept (like Unix or early network security) and curating its story.
